name: Kefir Mango Lassi
subtitle: Tangy probiotic kefir meets ripe Alphonso mangoes — the lassi upgrade
blrNote: Peak mango season is April–June. Badami mangoes from KR Market are ideal — they're Bengaluru's answer to Alphonso, with a rich, fragrant sweetness. Use pre-made milk kefir from your ongoing batch. Off-season, use frozen mango pulp from Nature's Basket or Amazon.

ingredients.name: Finished milk kefir
substitutions: Thick dahi / yogurt (less probiotic diversity but works)

IN.where: Make at home from milk kefir grains (see milk-kefir-basic recipe), or buy Milky Mist kefir from supermarkets.

ingredients.name: Ripe mango (Alphonso or Badami)
substitutions: Canned Alphonso pulp (Ratna or Kohinoor brand)

IN.where: KR Market, Hopcoms, any fruit vendor. Badami Apr–Jun, Alphonso Apr–May, Raspuri May–Jun.
Any kirana store or spice shop

ingredients.name: Honey or sugar
substitutions: Skip entirely if mango is very ripe and sweet

ingredients.name: Ice cubes
substitutions: Use frozen mango chunks instead for a thicker lassi

equipment.name: Blender or mixie
equipment.notes: Any standard Indian mixie jar works perfectly.

equipment.name: Tall glasses
equipment.notes: Serve in tall glasses for the classic lassi presentation.

tldr: Blend finished milk kefir with ripe mango, cardamom, and a touch of honey. A probiotic-rich twist on the classic mango lassi — tangy, creamy, and deeply satisfying.

steps.title: Prepare the kefir
steps.instruction: Use 300ml of finished milk kefir from your ongoing batch (see milk-kefir-basic recipe). If you don't have homemade kefir, store-bought plain kefir works. The kefir should be well-chilled from the fridge — cold kefir blends into a better lassi.
tips: A 24-hour kefir that's tangy but not over-fermented gives the best flavour balance with sweet mango.
If your kefir has separated slightly, just shake or stir it smooth before measuring.
Thick, well-fermented kefir makes a creamier lassi than thin, under-fermented kefir.
steps.checkpoint: 300ml of cold, smooth milk kefir measured and ready

steps.title: Blend with mango and spices
steps.instruction: Peel and chop the mango (or measure 200g of pulp). Add to the blender with the kefir, seeds from 2 cardamom pods, honey or sugar to taste, and ice cubes. Blend on high for 30–60 seconds until completely smooth and frothy.
tips: Taste before adding sweetener — ripe Badami or Alphonso mangoes are often sweet enough on their own.
Crush the cardamom seeds slightly before adding for maximum fragrance.
For a thicker, shake-like consistency, use frozen mango chunks instead of fresh + ice.
steps.checkpoint: Smooth, vibrant orange-yellow lassi with visible froth on top

steps.title: Serve chilled
steps.instruction: Pour into tall glasses. Optionally garnish with a pinch of cardamom powder, a few saffron strands, or thin mango slices. Serve immediately — the probiotic cultures are most active when fresh.
tips: Kefir mango lassi is best consumed fresh — the live cultures begin to change the flavour within a few hours.
If making in advance, store in the fridge for up to 4 hours. Stir before serving as it may separate slightly.
The tangy kefir cuts through the mango sweetness beautifully — if it's too tart, add more honey.
steps.checkpoint: Cold, frothy, vibrant mango kefir lassi in glasses, ready to drink

images.caption: Mango lassi — made with kefir for a probiotic upgrade
images.attribution: Asha Susan, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
images.alt: Glass of vibrant orange mango lassi topped with cardamom

culturalContext.story: Lassi is one of India's oldest beverages — a blend of dahi and water that has cooled the subcontinent for millennia. The mango lassi is a Punjabi speciality that became globally famous through Indian restaurants worldwide. This version replaces dahi with milk kefir, adding 50+ strains of probiotics to an already-beloved drink. It's a fusion that honours tradition while amplifying nutrition.
culturalContext.historicalNote: The word 'lassi' derives from the Hindi 'lassī,' which comes from the Sanskrit 'lakṣikā.' Lassi has been drunk in the Punjab region for thousands of years, originally as a simple salted buttermilk. The sweet mango version is a more recent (20th century) innovation that became a global ambassador for Indian cuisine.
culturalContext.significance: In Bengaluru, mango season (April–June) is a cultural event. Every household has its favourite variety and its favourite mango preparation. Replacing dahi with kefir in a mango lassi is a small change with big probiotic benefits — the tangy kefir actually complements mango better than mild dahi.
culturalContext.funFact: India produces over 50% of the world's mangoes. The Alphonso variety — considered the 'king of mangoes' — was named after Afonso de Albuquerque, a Portuguese general who introduced grafting techniques to Goa in the 1500s.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Seasonality matters
thingsToAccountFor.description: The best kefir mango lassi requires ripe, in-season mangoes (April–June in Bengaluru). Off-season mangoes lack the sweetness and aroma to balance kefir's tang. Use frozen pulp outside the season.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Don't heat the kefir
thingsToAccountFor.description: Never heat or cook the kefir — high temperatures kill the probiotic cultures. Always blend cold and serve immediately.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Kefir tang balance
thingsToAccountFor.description: If your kefir is very tangy (over-fermented), the lassi may taste too sour. Use a milder 12-hour kefir, or add extra sweetener to balance.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Not applicable — this is a fresh blended beverage best consumed immediately.
tips: Freeze kefir mango lassi into popsicle moulds for probiotic mango ice pops
Freeze in ice cube trays and blend later for a quick smoothie base

variations.name: Rose Kefir Lassi
variations.description: Replace mango with 1 tbsp rose syrup (rooh afza) and a handful of soaked sabja (basil) seeds. A fragrant, pink, Mughlai-inspired probiotic drink.

variations.name: Saffron Kefir Lassi
variations.description: Steep a generous pinch of saffron in 2 tbsp warm milk for 10 minutes. Blend into the kefir with sugar and a pinch of cardamom. Rich, golden, aromatic.

variations.name: Salted Kefir Lassi (Namkeen)
variations.description: Skip the mango and sweetener. Blend kefir with roasted cumin powder, black salt, and fresh mint. The savoury counterpart — essentially a probiotic chaas.
